definition: EBITDA addback practices inflate reported leverage by 15-40%, meaning a deal at reported 6x leverage may be 7.5-8.5x on actual cash EBITDA. At 9-10% all-in cost, 8x leverage implies interest expense consuming 70-80% of cash EBITDA. This raises the economy's effective stall speed to ~1.5-2.0% GDP.
evidence: ECB analysis and Fed flagging confirmed 15-40% addback range
At 9-10% all-in cost and 8x actual leverage, interest expense consumes 70-80% of cash EBITDA
pair_1 called this the most concrete and alarming claim
Underlying data from LCD and Refinitiv databases is well-established
open_questions: How widespread are addback distortions across the leveraged loan universe?
At what GDP growth rate does the distortion trigger a cascading default cycle?
Can regulators effectively constrain addback practices?
